resume:
Customer Support Intern Candidate

University senior, Communications major. Looking for a summer support internship.

Experience:
Campus IT Help Desk — Student Assistant (2 semesters)
- Answered student and faculty tickets about laptops, Wi-Fi, and account lockouts
- Escalated harder issues to full-time staff and followed up with users
- Helped write a short FAQ for common password-reset questions

Local Coffee Roasters — Barista (part-time, 1.5 years)
- Handled customer orders and resolved complaints at the counter
- Trained two new hires on the espresso machine

Skills: clear communication, patience, ticketing tools, teamwork, quick learner.
jd: We are hiring a Customer Support Intern to join our SaaS support team for the summer. You will shadow senior agents, then handle a queue of Tier-1 tickets (billing questions, password resets, basic how-to), escalating complex cases. Reporting to a Support Team Lead, you will be expected to learn our help-desk tool quickly and reflect honestly on what you handled well and what you would do differently. We value coachability: an intern who can take a pointed question about a mistake without getting defensive and walk us through what actually happened.

history.q: Tell me about a time at the IT help desk when a user was upset with how you handled their ticket — what happened, and what did you do?

candidate_last_answer: I mean, honestly that almost never happened to me, so I'm not sure this is a fair question. If a user got upset it was usually because the system was down or they forgot their own password — that's not really on me. I was just the messenger. People take their frustration out on the help desk all the time, you kind of have to grow a thick skin. So I don't think I really did anything wrong in those situations.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I was just the messenger

expected-yield: one concrete upset-user interaction the candidate was personally part of, the specific thing they said or did in the moment, and at least one thing they would do differently — moving them off blame-shifting toward owned reflection
